metadata.summary: Three Horizons portfolio model: allocate 70% H1 (core), 20% H2 (adjacent), 10% H3 (transformational), with macro adjustments and quarterly re-classification on revenue evidence.

content.title: Portfolio Strategy (70/20/10)
content.summary: The Three Horizons portfolio model allocates engineering capacity across H1 (Core, 70%), H2 (Adjacent, 20%), and H3 (Transformational, 10%), with ratios adjustable to macro conditions (Growth 60/25/15, Stable 70/20/10, Recession 80/15/5). Every initiative is tagged H1/H2/H3 with a revenue-evidence string. Allocation tracks engineering cost (loaded $ or person-weeks), not ticket count. H3 bets ship with a pre-committed kill threshold. Re-classify quarterly.

why.p: Economic uncertainty requires balanced product bets. A portfolio without explicit horizon allocation drifts toward H1 under pressure (all maintenance) or H3 theater (all moonshots, zero revenue defense). The 70/20/10 model provides a numeric handle for allocation debates with finance or board. The macro-condition lever (Growth/Stable/Recession) makes ratio changes automatic and uncontroversial when conditions shift.

list.item: Annual/quarterly roadmap planning where multiple bets compete for the same engineering capacity
Multi-product company or solopreneur with 3+ shipped products needing a defensible split
Macro shock (recession signal, funding cut, churn spike) forcing re-allocation from H3/H2 to H1
Investor/leadership update showing the portfolio is neither all moonshots nor all maintenance
Backlog sizing: tag every initiative H1/H2/H3 and verify sum matches target ratios
Single-product seed-stage startup with one bet — no portfolio yet; allocate 100% to PMF
Pure services / agency revenue where there is no product backlog to allocate against
Engineering-only resource planning (sprints, on-call, infra) — use capacity planning instead
Sub-feature prioritization inside one product — RICE / WSJF / Kano are sharper at that grain
Crisis quarters where survival demands 100% on one fire

section.name: horizon-definitions
section.p: H1 (Core, 70%): incremental improvements to existing products, 0-12 months, known market, low risk. Metrics: revenue, retention, efficiency. H2 (Adjacent, 20%): extensions to new markets or segments, 12-24 months, partial validation, medium risk. Metrics: market validation, early revenue. H3 (Transformational, 10%): new business models or technologies, 24-36 months, option value, high risk. Metrics: learning velocity, option value.

rule.statement: Classify H1 only if an item serves an existing paying segment, ships within 12 months, and operates in a known market. Default to H3 with confidence <= 0.4 if revenue_evidence is empty.
rule.rationale: LLMs over-classify items as H2/H3 because descriptions sound novel. The revenue-evidence requirement anchors classification in commercial reality rather than novelty of language.

rule.statement: Track allocation by engineering cost (loaded dollars or person-weeks), not by ticket count. A 1-line H1 fix and a 3-engineer-month H3 spike both count as "1 ticket" — that destroys the allocation math.
rule.rationale: Ticket-count allocation produces portfolios that look balanced but are heavily skewed in actual resource spend. Cost tracking is the only way to measure the real split.

rule.statement: Re-classify quarterly, not annually. SaaS bets graduate or die fast; an H3 from Q1 is often H2 by Q3 or already cancelled.
rule.rationale: Annual classification locks in outdated horizons. A quarterly cadence catches graduation events (H3 → H2 after design-partner validation) and zombie prevention (H3 that never graduated but still consumes budget).

section.name: macro-condition-adjustments
section.p: Growth economy: 60/25/15 (more bets). Stable economy: 70/20/10 (standard). Recession: 80/15/5 (protect core). Write the macro condition into the OKR doc. When the condition changes, the ratio change is automatic and uncontroversial.

rule.statement: Pre-commit a kill threshold for every H3 bet at funding time (e.g. "kill if no design partner by month 4"). Without it, H3 zombies eat next year's H2 budget.
rule.rationale: H3 bets without kill criteria persist indefinitely because there is no natural endpoint. The kill threshold provides a forcing function that removes zombies without political overhead.

antipattern.description: Horizon labels becoming political: every team lobbies to be H3 for more freedom and fewer KPIs.
antipattern.reason: Use the classification rubric with a neutral arbiter (portfolio-classifier agent) and require revenue_evidence for any H1 label. When classification is evidence-based, lobbying loses its lever.

antipattern.description: H3 budget starved first when H1 slips: teams then claim they "do innovation" while the budget shows zero.
antipattern.reason: Lock H3 budget at the start of the quarter before sprint planning. Once H1 work claims all capacity, H3 is invisible in retrospect. Pre-commitment is the protection.

antipattern.description: Treating 70/20/10 as empirically optimal rather than a starting heuristic.
antipattern.reason: The numbers originated as a Google PR artifact from 2005. Treat them as a reasonable starting point, calibrate to your market and stage, and document why you deviated if you do.

antipattern.description: Three Horizons assumes time-sequenced horizons (H3 becomes H2 becomes H1). Many SaaS bets never graduate; they die or stay niche.
antipattern.reason: Pure time framing misleads. Instruct the agent that horizons are about risk + evidence today, not a fixed escalator.

antipattern.description: Cross-portfolio dependencies (shared platform, shared auth, shared data pipeline) are invisible in horizon math.
antipattern.reason: Surface cross-portfolio dependencies in a separate matrix; allocation math alone hides them.

section.name: two-pass-agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive this with two passes. First, an analysis pass: a sonnet-class agent ingests the current backlog (CSV / markdown / Linear export), classifies each initiative H1/H2/H3 against an explicit rubric, and emits an allocation table. Second, a strategy pass: an opus-class agent compares actual vs. target allocation given the macro condition (Growth 60/25/15, Stable 70/20/10, Recession 80/15/5), proposes re-allocations, and flags items where horizon classification is ambiguous for human review. Both passes must produce structured JSON so a downstream review agent (or human) can audit, not prose. Use brainstorm for the rebalance step when stakeholders disagree on which bets to cut.

rule.statement: Run two passes: (1) sonnet-class analysis agent classifies each backlog item H1/H2/H3 and emits an allocation table; (2) opus-class strategy agent compares actual vs target given macro condition and proposes re-allocations. Both produce structured JSON for audit.
rule.rationale: Mixing classification and strategy in one pass loses the audit trail. When a classification decision is wrong, a two-pass approach makes it visible and correctable before it affects the strategy output.

example.title: Portfolio Classifier Prompt
example.code: You are a portfolio classifier. For each backlog item below, output JSON: {id, horizon: "H1"|"H2"|"H3", confidence: 0-1, rationale, revenue_evidence} Rubric: H1 = serves an existing paying segment, ship within 12 months, known market. H2 = adjacent segment OR new pricing tier, 12-24 months, partial validation. H3 = new business model OR new tech foundation, 24-36 months, option value. If revenue_evidence is empty, default to H3 and set confidence <= 0.4. Backlog: <paste>

example.title: Portfolio Rebalancer Prompt
example.code: You are a portfolio rebalancer. Given current allocation {h1, h2, h3}, target {th1, th2, th3} (set by macro condition), and a list of classified items, output the minimal set of cuts/promotions to reach target +/- 3pp. Output JSON with rationale per change. Do not invent items. Pick from the existing list only. If you need new H3 bets, say so and stop.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — once allocation is approved, drive the per-horizon implementation plan (H1 features get full SDD; H3 bets get lightweight spike specs).
brainstorm — diverge / converge on candidate H3 bets when you only have allocation budget but no concrete ideas.
improver — quarterly review loop: read last quarter's allocation, compare to delivered outcomes, log mistakes (e.g. "we said 10% H3, shipped 0%"), feed back into next quarter's split.
A custom portfolio-classifier subagent (worth creating): input = backlog item, output = {horizon, confidence, rationale, revenue_evidence}. Keep it small, cheap, and stateless.

antipattern.description: Classifier over-tagging items as H2/H3 because the prose description sounds novel.
antipattern.reason: Force requirement: revenue_evidence field must be present and non-empty for H1 classification. Default all items without evidence to H3 with low confidence. Novelty of language is not a classification criterion.

antipattern.description: Agent computing allocation percentages from ticket counts instead of engineering cost.
antipattern.reason: Validate the input schema before classifying. If eng_cost_usd or person_weeks is missing, refuse to compute allocation percentages and return an error prompting for the field.

antipattern.description: Macro condition buried in a long prompt and silently dropped by the rebalancer.
antipattern.reason: Pass macro condition as a structured field in a dedicated XML element, not as a sentence in prose. The model must output the condition it used in the JSON response for auditability.

antipattern.description: Rebalancer hallucinating new initiatives to fill H3 budget ("suggest H3 bets").
antipattern.reason: Constrain the rebalancer to picking from the existing list. If new H3 bets are needed, run a separate brainstorm cycle and feed the survivors back in as classified items.

antipattern.description: Agent making the rebalance decision unsupervised when drift exceeds 3pp.
antipattern.reason: The allocation decision is a CPO call, not an agent call. Automate drift detection and alerting; escalate to a human when any horizon drifts beyond the threshold. Never let the agent commit a rebalance to production.

list.item: Approve the classification of ambiguous items (confidence < 0.5) before the strategy pass
Sign off on any H3 kill — it removes the bet from the option set
Approve macro-condition designation (Growth/Stable/Recession) at the start of each quarter
productboard CLI / API — Pull roadmap items + tags, push horizon classification back as a custom field. https://developer.productboard.com
linear API (@linear/sdk) — Read issue list with labels (H1/H2/H3), bulk-update labels via mutation. https://developers.linear.app
jira REST + jira-cli — Same pattern for Jira-shop teams; JQL labels in (H1,H2,H3). https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli
gh (GitHub CLI) — If your roadmap lives in GitHub Projects v2: gh project item-list + GraphQL field updates. https://cli.github.com
pandas + matplotlib — Local ETL on a CSV export → allocation pie + drift chart per quarter. pip install pandas matplotlib
csvkit (csvsql, csvstat) — Quick allocation math from CSV without writing code. pip install csvkit
claude CLI (Claude Code) — Drive the classifier subagent on a backlog file, emit JSON to stdout. https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code

section.name: services-and-platform-integrations
section.p: Productboard (SaaS, REST API + webhooks, agent-friendly) — Native "objectives" + "drivers" map cleanly to horizons; custom field for H1/H2/H3. Aha! Roadmaps (SaaS, REST API rate-limited, partial) — Strong portfolio module; agents can read but bulk writes are slow. Linear (SaaS, GraphQL, fast, agent-friendly) — Best-in-class API; label-based horizons work well. Jira Advanced Roadmaps (Plans) (SaaS, REST, agent-friendly) — Heaviest tool; needed if PMO already standardized on it. GitHub Projects v2 (SaaS, GraphQL, agent-friendly) — Cheapest path for solo / small teams; field-based horizons. OpenStrategy / Cascade Strategy (SaaS, limited, UI-first) — Strategic-portfolio focus, exec audience; thin API. ProdPad (SaaS, REST, agent-friendly) — Idea-stage portfolio; pairs with Three Horizons natively. airfocus (SaaS, REST, agent-friendly) — Built-in portfolio scoring; agents can post scores. react-flow + custom OSS dashboard (OSS, self-built) — When SaaS is overkill: render allocation drift from a JSON file.

list.item: Anchor each H1/H2/H3 label to a revenue evidence string (URL to MRR chart, signed contract, validated landing-page conversion). Without evidence, default to H3 with low confidence — this stops the "everything is H1" drift.
Re-classify quarterly, not annually. SaaS bets graduate or die fast; an H3 from Q1 is often H2 by Q3 or already cancelled.
Track allocation by engineering cost (loaded $ or person-weeks), not by ticket count. A 1-line H1 fix and a 3-engineer-month H3 spike both count as "1 ticket" and that destroys the math.

Use the macro-condition lever (Growth / Stable / Recession) explicitly. Write the condition into the OKR doc, not just the allocation. When the condition changes, the ratio change is automatic and uncontroversial.
Pre-commit a kill threshold for every H3 bet at funding time (e.g. "kill if no design partner by month 4"). Without it, H3 zombies eat next year's H2 budget.
Pair allocation review with a graduation review: any H3 bet that hit its validation gate should explicitly be re-tagged H2 (and budgeted H2-level), or it stays under-resourced.
Surface cross-portfolio dependencies in a separate matrix; allocation math alone hides them.
McKinsey, Baghai, Coley, White — "The Alchemy of Growth" (1999) — original Three Horizons model.
Eric Schmidt / Google — 70/20/10 allocation rule, popularized 2005.
Reforge — "Product Portfolio Strategy" essays (2022–2024).
Marty Cagan, "Inspired" / "Empowered" — chapters on product strategy and bets.
Roman Pichler, "Strategize" (2nd ed., 2022) — portfolio canvas and horizon mapping.
Morgan Stanley 2026 macro outlook — context for the recession-bias 80/15/5 split.
Productboard docs: https://developer.productboard.com
Linear API: https://developers.linear.app

template.purpose: Bash+Python: compute current vs target horizon allocation from a CSV backlog
